quotes.verbatim_quote:
In Blues No. I for the first four bars I’m using an Emi7 run. You know that the chord for these four bars is G
major for three and G7 for one. But Emi7 and Gma6 are the same (relatives), so it can be used as a major run
(See Lesson 19). The same rule that applies to chords applies to runs.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the chords-with-two-names principle from Lesson 19 into the solo domain: a minor-7 run substitutes freely for its relative major chord, the central improvisational license of Baker's method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In Blues progression some-
times at the ninth and tenth bar we use the V7 for one bar (which is D7 in the key of G major), and the IV7
for one bar (which is C7 in the key of G major), this allows you to use an Ami7 run for one bar and a Gmi7 run
for one bar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the V7/IV7 substitution Baker plants at bars 9-10 of the blues and explains how the corresponding ii-min7 runs (Ami7 for D7, Gmi7 for C7) sit on those bars — the workhorse blues-reharm move.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice
that when connecting a dominant chord to a major the dominant in most of the time altered, (G7#529 to Cma
ot G135¢9 to Cma). By altering chords like this they blend together perfectly. This same thing applies to solo
work.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the always-alter-the-V-into-I rule, a load-bearing voice-leading principle that bridges Baker's chord substitutions to his run choices.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Whenever you have one or two bars of D7 going into G major, you may use an Ami7 run to a Cmi7 run
which will blend right into a G major run.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the concrete Ami7 → Cmi7 → G major run substitution recipe over V → I, one of the most characteristic Baker improvisational moves and a template for transposing the trick to any key.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the last two bars we have a Bmi7 run which is a relative to Gma7.
Bmi7 and Gma7 are such close relatives that one can be used for the other at any time.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Adds the iii-min7 / I-maj7 relative pair to the substitution toolkit, broadening the relative-substitution rule beyond vi-min7 / I-maj6.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now this is something that can be done in any song
blues, ballad, or bounce tune.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Generalizes the altered-V and relative-substitution moves beyond the blues form to ballads and bounce tunes, marking these as cross-genre vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
in the last two bars we have what I call a riff run. Riffs can be used any place, but we're not ready to
o into riffs yet, so you just accept it for what it is now.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the riff-run category as a distinct device that lives outside the chord-derived run system, foreshadowing later material while flagging it as exception territory.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
All of this material is in G
major and it should be transposed to the keys of: A’, Bé, C, Dé, E4 and F major. The best thing to do is to set
aside a whole day for each key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the one-day-per-key practice schedule for transposing Lesson 31 across six keys, an unusually concrete prescription for how to convert single-key knowledge into universal recall.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I want you to take the same runs that we have
in Blues No. 1, 2 and 3. Then change them around in as many different ways as you can possibly think of, just as

I have done.

You should be able to make up at least three more solos for each one of these blue examples.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets a quantified composition exercise (three new solos per blues example) that operationalizes the transition from imitation to original idea generation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
After you've learned the connections by heart, practice strumming the chords and humming the runs to yourself, By
doing this, you can hear just how these runs sound against the chord progressions. Then take the same runs and
try to make new ideas.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the strum-and-hum drill for internalizing run-against-chord sound, the connective tissue between the harmonic substitutions and the melodic vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote: All you have to do is to use different tempos, and timings, and you will get new effects.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names tempo and timing variation as the cheapest, most reliable ways to generate fresh material from existing runs — a phrasing-development rule of thumb.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This solo is in the key of G major, and should be played between the 7th and 12th frets on your guitar. With
this information you should very easily find the proper fingering.

After you work this solo out in G major, transpose it to the keys of D?, E¢, F, Aé and Be,
In each of these keys you are to use the same fingering. You only change the position.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pins the Lesson 36 solo to a specific neck region (7th-12th fret) and reasserts that transposition is achieved purely by position-shift with identical fingering, the bedrock mechanical rule of the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If you will notice I've written two or three bars, and in some places the bars
are blank. This of course is where you fill in. Over these blank bars I have
written the type of run I'd like you to use, so all you have to do is fill
them in.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Describes the scaffolded-improvisation exercise format Baker uses in Lesson 37: notated bars interleaved with labeled blank bars, training the student to generate phrases that match a specified run-type.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Above all, don’t just throw the runs together. Make sure
that they blend together as well as those that I have written.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the over-arching phrasing aesthetic Baker insists on: connection and blending of runs, not concatenation — the criterion separating mechanical execution from musical solo.
